statements.sql: INSERT INTO silver.PivotTarget (MetricName, RedCount, BlackCount, SilverCount) SELECT 'product_counts', ISNULL([Red], 0), ISNULL([Black], 0), ISNULL([Silver], 0) FROM (SELECT Color, ProductID FROM bronze.Product) AS src PIVOT (COUNT(ProductID) FOR Color IN ([Red], [Black], [Silver])) AS p

refactor.extracted_sql: SELECT 'product_counts', ISNULL([Red], 0), ISNULL([Black], 0), ISNULL([Silver], 0) FROM ( SELECT Color, ProductID FROM bronze.Product ) AS src PIVOT ( COUNT(ProductID) FOR Color IN ([Red], [Black], [Silver]) ) AS p
refactor.refactored_sql: WITH import_product AS ( SELECT Color, ProductID FROM bronze.Product ), color_values AS ( SELECT 'Red' AS color UNION ALL SELECT 'Black' UNION ALL SELECT 'Silver' ), color_counts AS ( SELECT cv.color, COUNT(ip.ProductID) AS count FROM color_values cv LEFT JOIN import_product ip ON cv.color = ip.Color GROUP BY cv.color ), final AS ( SELECT 'product_counts' AS MetricName, ISNULL(MAX(CASE WHEN color = 'Red' THEN count END), 0) AS RedCount, ISNULL(MAX(CASE WHEN color = 'Black' THEN count END), 0) AS BlackCount, ISNULL(MAX(CASE WHEN color = 'Silver' THEN count END), 0) AS SilverCount FROM color_counts ) SELECT * FROM final

source_tables.summary: Both queries source from bronze.Product. Original uses inline subquery; refactored uses CTE. Semantically identical source.

output_columns.summary: Both produce 4 columns with counts by color plus metric label. Column order and data types match the target table schema (MetricName, RedCount, BlackCount, SilverCount). Column mapping by INSERT position ensures equivalence.

joins.summary: Original PIVOT implicitly ensures all IN-clause colors appear. Refactored uses LEFT JOIN with color_values CTE to guarantee all three colors (Red, Black, Silver) are represented. Both semantically equivalent in ensuring complete color coverage.

filters.summary: No WHERE clauses in either query. Both restrict to specified colors (Red, Black, Silver) via PIVOT IN clause (original) or LEFT JOIN (refactored). Identical filtering.

aggregation_grain.summary: Both aggregate COUNT(ProductID) per color and produce one output row with all three color counts as columns. Aggregation grain and result set size are identical.
